questions.question: What is an Agile Release Train (ART) in SAFe?
options: A long-lived, self-organizing team-of-teams (typically 5–12 teams, 50–125 people) aligned to a value stream on a shared cadence
A single Scrum team that releases software weekly
A temporary project team disbanded after each release
The portfolio-level funding mechanism for value streams
questions.explanation: The ART is the central construct of SAFe (Essential level): a long-lived team-of-teams (5–12 teams, 50–125 people) aligned to a value stream, planning together every Planning Interval.

questions.question: In SAFe 6.0, what does the acronym "PI" now stand for?
options: Planning Interval
Program Increment
Product Iteration
Portfolio Initiative
questions.explanation: SAFe 6.0 (2023) renamed "Program Increment" to "Planning Interval" — the acronym PI stayed, but the words changed to emphasize a fixed planning cadence (typically 8–12 weeks). Saying "Program Increment" signals SAFe 5.x.

questions.question: During PI Planning, risks are categorized with ROAM. What does ROAM stand for?
options: Resolved, Owned, Accepted, Mitigated
Reviewed, Ordered, Assigned, Measured
Raised, Observed, Analyzed, Managed
Ranked, Owned, Accepted, Monitored
questions.explanation: ROAM = Resolved (no longer a concern), Owned (someone takes responsibility), Accepted (live with it), Mitigated (a plan reduces it). Every risk is tagged so the ART leaves with no un-triaged risk.

questions.question: PI Planning is a two-day cadence event for the whole ART, held every Planning Interval.
questions.explanation: PI Planning is the signature SAFe event — a two-day, whole-ART planning event held every PI, typically facilitated by the Release Train Engineer.

questions.question: What is the confidence-vote threshold for an ART to commit to its PI Objectives?
options: A fist-of-five average of 3 or more
A unanimous vote of 5
A simple majority of yes votes
An average of 2 or more
questions.explanation: Teams vote fist-of-five on confidence in their PI Objectives. An average of 3+ lets management accept the commitment; below 3 the team reworks the plan. Anyone voting 1 or 2 is invited to explain.

questions.question: What are the four SAFe configurations, from smallest to most complete?
options: Essential, Large Solution, Portfolio, Full
Team, Program, Portfolio, Enterprise
Basic, Standard, Advanced, Complete
Essential, Portfolio, Solution, Enterprise
questions.explanation: Essential (Team + ART, the indispensable core), Large Solution (adds the Solution Train), Portfolio (adds Lean Portfolio Management), and Full (all levels). Start at Essential and add levels only as the problem demands.

questions.question: What are the four SAFe Core Values in 6.0?
options: Alignment, Transparency, Respect for People, Relentless Improvement
Alignment, Built-in Quality, Transparency, Program Execution
Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect
Trust, Commitment, Accountability, Results
questions.explanation: In 6.0 the four Core Values are Alignment, Transparency, Respect for People, and Relentless Improvement. Built-in Quality and Program Execution (the 5.x values) were dropped — Built-in Quality is now a practice. (Option 3 is the Scrum values; option 4 is Lencioni.)

questions.question: How does SAFe define WSJF and Cost of Delay?
options: WSJF = Cost of Delay / Job Size, where CoD = User-Business Value + Time Criticality + Risk Reduction/Opportunity Enablement
WSJF = Job Size / Cost of Delay, where CoD = Reach × Impact
WSJF = Value − Effort, with no Cost of Delay term
WSJF = User-Business Value × Time Criticality
questions.explanation: WSJF = Cost of Delay ÷ Job Size, where CoD = User-Business Value + Time Criticality + Risk Reduction/Opportunity Enablement. It favors small jobs with high cost of delay.

questions.question: Feature X: Value 8, Time Criticality 5, RR/OE 8, Job Size 3. Feature Y: Value 13, Time Criticality 2, RR/OE 3, Job Size 13. Which goes first by WSJF?
options: Feature X — its smaller job size and higher CoD give the far higher WSJF (7.0 vs 1.4)
Feature Y — it has the highest raw user-business value
They tie, so pick either
Feature Y — larger jobs always rank higher in WSJF
questions.explanation: X scores WSJF 7.0 vs Y's 1.4. Despite Y's higher raw value, X's smaller job size and higher cost of delay win — exactly the point of WSJF: small, high-delay-cost jobs first.

questions.question: In SAFe, how do Product Management and the Product Owner differ?
options: Product Management works at the ART level (ART Backlog, Features, Vision); the PO works at the team level (Team Backlog, Stories, accepts work)
They are the same role at different companies
Product Management owns Stories; the PO owns Features
The PO works at the portfolio level; Product Management at the team level
questions.explanation: Product Management owns the ART Backlog and defines Features, Vision, and Roadmap (the "what" for the train). Product Owners own the Team Backlog and Stories (the "what" for one team). Features decompose into Stories.

questions.question: In SAFe 6.0, the ART Sync umbrella event combines which two syncs?
options: Coach Sync (Scrum Masters/Team Coaches + RTE) and PO Sync (POs + Product Management)
Daily Scrum and Sprint Review
Portfolio Sync and Solution Sync
System Demo and Inspect & Adapt
questions.explanation: ART Sync = Coach Sync (coordinates dependencies, progress, impediments) + PO Sync (tracks progress to PI Objectives, scope, priorities).

questions.question: SAFe 6.0 retired the "House of Lean" and now defines the Lean-Agile Mindset as the five principles of Lean Thinking plus the Agile Manifesto.
questions.explanation: The House of Lean was retired in 6.0; the Lean-Agile Mindset is now Lean Thinking + the Agile Manifesto. This is a common "are you on 5.x or 6.0?" tell.

questions.question: A PI Planning risk can't be eliminated, but the team puts a plan in place to reduce its probability and impact. Which ROAM category applies?
options: Mitigated
Resolved
Accepted
Owned
questions.explanation: Mitigated = a plan reduces the risk. Resolved = no longer a concern; Accepted = nothing more can be done, live with it; Owned = a person takes responsibility but it's still unresolved.

questions.question: When is LeSS the better fit over SAFe?
options: When you want to keep Scrum pure and descale with very few new roles — best at 2–8 teams sharing one PO, one backlog, one Sprint
When you need portfolio governance and lean budgets across many trains
When your dominant pain is cross-team integration at 3–9 teams
When you want a two-day PI Planning event for 100+ people
questions.explanation: LeSS keeps Scrum pure and descales with minimal new roles (2–8 teams, one PO/backlog/Sprint). Cross-team integration at 3–9 teams points to Nexus; portfolio governance across many trains points to SAFe.

questions.question: The Spotify model (squads, tribes, chapters, guilds) is an official, off-the-shelf scaling framework.
questions.explanation: It is a 2012 snapshot of how Spotify was organized at one moment — never a framework, and Spotify itself moved past it. Copying the org chart without the autonomy-with-alignment culture is a cargo-cult anti-pattern.

questions.question: A lead wants to adopt "Full SAFe" on day one for a 60-person, single-train product with no portfolio-governance needs. What is the best advice?
options: Start at Essential SAFe — a single ~60-person train needs only Team + ART; Full imports Large Solution and Portfolio weight with no matching problem
Adopt Full SAFe so all levels are ready in advance
Skip SAFe entirely and use only Scrum with no coordination
Adopt Portfolio SAFe to get lean budgets immediately
questions.explanation: Use the smallest correct configuration: a single train with no multi-ART solution or portfolio funding problem needs only Essential (Team + ART). Full prematurely adds Large Solution and Portfolio roles/events with no problem to solve.

questions.question: Which event closes a Planning Interval and drives ART-level improvement?
options: Inspect and Adapt — the PI System Demo, a measurement review, and a structured problem-solving workshop
The Sprint Retrospective
PI Planning Day 2
The Daily Coach Sync
questions.explanation: Inspect and Adapt (I&A) is the ART's retrospective: the PI System Demo of the full integrated solution, a quantitative/qualitative measurement review, and a root-cause problem-solving workshop that produces improvement items for the next PI.

questions.question: Which SAFe Lean-Agile Principle most directly underpins PI Planning?
options: Principle 7 — Apply cadence, synchronize with cross-domain planning
Principle 1 — Take an economic view
Principle 10 — Organize around value
Principle 6 — Make value flow without interruptions
questions.explanation: Principle 7 (cadence + cross-domain synchronization) is exactly what PI Planning operationalizes. Principle 1 underpins WSJF; Principle 10 underpins value streams and ARTs.
